title: Festival dumplings
subtitle: Jamaican sweet fried cornmeal dumplings
excerpt: Sweet, golden-fried Jamaican dumplings made from cornmeal and flour, slightly crisp outside and soft within. Festival are the classic companion to escovitch fish and jerk chicken at Jamaican roadside stalls.
sourceNotes: Festival are a distinctly Jamaican fried dumpling, sweeter and softer than the plain boiled or fried dumplings (Johnny cakes) found elsewhere in the Caribbean. The addition of cornmeal gives a slight graininess to the texture and a golden colour. The name likely comes from their festive character as a treat sold at outdoor events and markets. They are most commonly sold at jerk stalls alongside jerk chicken and escovitch fish, where the sweetness of the dumpling balances the heat and acidity of the main dish.

content.text: Festival are the sweet fried dumplings that turn up at every Jamaican roadside jerk stall, nestled in the container beside the chicken and the escovitch fish. They are not dessert, the sweetness is gentle, there to cut through the salt and heat of the main dish. The cornmeal gives them a slightly grainy texture and a deep golden colour when fried.

The dough is simple: flour, cornmeal, sugar, baking powder, a little butter, and milk to bring it together. Shape the dumplings into short, tapered cylinders or ovals, not round balls, which take longer to cook through. Fry in enough oil to half-submerge them so each side can brown fully.

items.name: Whole milk
items.prepNote: plus a little more if needed

content.text: Combine the {{plain-flour}} of flour, {{polenta}} of cornmeal, {{caster-sugar}} of sugar, and {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt in a large bowl. Rub in the {{unsalted-butter}} of cold butter until the mixture resembles coarse breadcrumbs. Add the {{whole-milk}} of milk a little at a time, mixing until the dough just comes together. It should be soft but not sticky, and hold its shape when pressed. Knead gently for 1 minute.
Divide the dough into 8 to 10 equal pieces, each about 50 g. Roll each piece into a short oval or tapered cylinder roughly 8 cm long, like a small sausage with pointed ends. Flatten slightly.

Heat the {{vegetable-oil}} of oil in a saucepan over medium heat to 170°C. If you don't have a thermometer, test with a small piece of dough. It should sizzle steadily without browning immediately. Fry the dumplings in batches of 3 to 4 for 3 to 4 minutes per side, turning once, until deep golden all over and cooked through. Lift out with a slotted spoon and drain on kitchen paper.

items.fix: Lower the heat to medium-low. Festival need time to cook through; 3 to 4 minutes per side at a moderate temperature is better than 2 minutes at high heat
items.cause: Oil too hot; frying too fast
items.symptom: Dumplings brown outside but raw inside

items.fix: Dust your hands and the work surface with flour. Add flour a tablespoon at a time until the dough holds its shape without sticking
Knead gently for 1 minute only. Add a teaspoon of baking powder to the dry ingredients next time for a lighter result
items.cause: Dough over-kneaded; not enough baking powder

items.fix: The dough needs to hold its shape when rolled. If it tears, knead for another minute and check the surface doesn't crack when shaped
items.cause: Dough too wet or not kneaded enough to develop structure
items.symptom: Dumplings falling apart in the oil

content.text: Festival are associated with the Jamaican roadside stall rather than the home kitchen, where plain boiled or fried flour dumplings are more common. The cornmeal and the sugar make them a slightly special thing, a side that pairs specifically with the intense flavours of the jerk stall. At home, they are made for parties and celebrations rather than everyday meals. They are best eaten within an hour of frying; the sweetness and lightness diminish as they sit.
